name: Julia Revise.jl
summary: Library for hot code update during interactive Julia development. When source files change on disk, Revise re-parses, incrementally updates the method table (adds/replaces/edits methods), and invalidates compilation caches for affected methods. The next call to a changed method triggers recompilation. Method tables are first-class runtime objects — that's why this works.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Julia's **Revise.jl** (Tim Holy) is a library, not a language feature, that provides hot code update during interactive development. When source files change on disk, Revise re-parses, incrementally updates the method table (adding new methods, replacing edited ones), and invalidates compilation caches for affected methods. The next call to a changed method triggers recompilation.

sections.title: How it works
sections.content:
The trick that makes this work is Julia's method table being a first-class runtime object — methods are values that can be added, removed, or replaced. Combined with Julia's JIT, this means the running program always executes whatever the latest source says, without a restart.

Revise is the central tool of Julia's interactive development workflow: open a REPL, edit code, see changes immediately. The analogous design in Common Lisp is the ancestor; Julia proves the pattern works in a modern language with aggressive compilation.

The limitation is the same one Common Lisp faces: existing references to method-defined values continue to point at the *old* objects, while new lookups find the *new* ones. For short-lived interactive sessions this is rarely a problem; for long-running services with hot reload as a deployment mechanism, more discipline is needed.
